era_context: Fourth Dynasty Egypt under Khufu; Great Pyramid of Giza in active construction. Mesopotamia in Early Dynastic Period III. Indus Valley in Early Harappan phase. This year represents the height of Old Kingdom Egyptian power and monumental achievement.
geographic_coverage_gaps: East Asia beyond the Indus Valley

events.title: Great Pyramid of Giza construction in progress
events.description: The Great Pyramid continues under construction during Khufu's reign. The project involves quarrying limestone from the Giza Plateau and transporting it to the construction site. Workers, likely a combination of conscripted labour and skilled craftsmen, place the blocks with remarkable precision. The internal chambers and passages are being constructed or completed. The pyramid's construction represents an unprecedented mobilisation of resources and labour, reflecting the centralised power of the Egyptian state.
events.certainty_note: The pyramid's existence and Khufu's attribution are archaeologically confirmed. The precise year of construction is uncertain (±5–10 years).

events.title: Egyptian state mobilisation of labour and resources for monumental construction
events.description: The construction of the Great Pyramid requires the mobilisation of vast labour forces, quarrying operations, and supply chains. The state controls the distribution of food, tools, and materials. Workers are housed in temporary settlements near the construction site. The project demonstrates the state's capacity to extract resources from the broader economy and concentrate them on a single monumental goal. This mobilisation is supported by a sophisticated administrative system and ideological legitimacy rooted in the pharaoh's divine status.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Giza, including worker settlements and tool marks

events.certainty_note: The existence of labour mobilisation is confirmed by archaeological evidence. Specific details for 2562 BCE are not documented.
cross_references: Part of broader Old Kingdom economic system

events.title: Mesopotamian Early Dynastic Period III: city-state conflicts and trade
events.description: In southern Mesopotamia, city-states continue to compete for resources and territory. Irrigation agriculture supports dense urban populations. Cuneiform writing is used for administrative, religious, and commercial purposes. Trade networks extend to the Persian Gulf, the Indus Valley, and beyond. The period is characterised by political fragmentation and frequent conflicts, which will eventually lead to the rise of the Akkadian Empire.
key_figures: Rulers of Lagash, Uruk, and Ur (names and dates uncertain)
events.certainty_note: The ED III period is confirmed archaeologically. Specific events for 2562 BCE are not documented. Mesopotamian chronology remains uncertain.
cross_references: Part of Early Dynastic Period III (c. 2600–2334 BCE)

events.title: Indus Valley Early Harappan phase: settlement expansion and standardisation
events.description: The Early Harappan phase continues, with settlements expanding and showing increasing standardisation in pottery, tools, and settlement patterns. Trade connections with Mesopotamia and the Persian Gulf are evident. The proto-urban centres are developing the social and economic structures that will characterise the Mature Harappan phase.

sources.name: Archaeological excavations at Indus Valley sites

events.certainty_note: The Early Harappan phase is archaeologically confirmed. Specific events for 2562 BCE are not documented.
cross_references: Part of Early Harappan phase (c. 3300–2600 BCE)

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence identified for events listed. The chronological uncertainties noted for previous years apply equally to 2562 BCE.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 2562 BCE is similar to that for 2560–2561 BCE. The surviving sources are heavily weighted toward Egypt and monumental contexts. The lives of ordinary people and non-elite settlements are largely invisible.

graph_edges.note: Indus Valley Early Harappan development continues
